title: Piccalilli
subtitle: The proper British mustard pickle.
excerpt: Cauliflower, courgette, beans, and shallots brined overnight then cooked into a turmeric-yellow mustard sauce thickened with cornflour. Yields about 1.5 kg, three jars. Three weeks in the cupboard before opening; six months on the shelf once sealed.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), recipe for Indian Pickle (the name Beeton uses for piccalilli), Project Gutenberg #10136. Beeton's version is brined for a week and uses whole spices; the modern shorter brine plus ground spices produces a cleaner, brighter pickle. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), pickle chapter, gives the cornflour-thickened mustard sauce that defines piccalilli against other vegetable pickles. The technique of bringing the pickle to a brief boil with the vegetables raw rather than pre-cooked is drawn from the published preserving advice of the National Center for Home Food Preservation (NCHFP), public-domain US government guidance.

content.text: Piccalilli is the British mustard pickle. It mixes cauliflower florets, courgette, green beans, and shallots into a yellow turmeric sauce stiff with English mustard. Three weeks in the cupboard mellows the bite. The result is sweet-sharp and slightly hot. It goes with cold ham, pork pies, ploughman's lunches, and Boxing Day leftovers.

Two things separate piccalilli from other British vegetable pickles. The first is the salt brine: the vegetables sit overnight in a strong brine that draws water out and keeps them crisp through the cook. The second is the sauce, thickened with cornflour rather than reduced by long simmering, so the vegetables stay close to their raw texture inside a thick mustard glaze.

The vegetables can flex by season. A glut of cauliflower in early autumn is the classic; runner beans, marrow, or shallots can stand in or pad out the mix. Cucumber adds crunch in late summer. What matters is the total weight of vegetables and that none of them is so soft it collapses in the brine, beetroot, tomato, and aubergine all do, and don't belong here.

attrs.body: The salt brine pulls water out of the vegetables before the vinegar goes near them. Without it, the vegetables release their own water into the finished pickle and the sauce thins to a soup over three weeks in the jar. A short brine of twelve hours is enough; anything over twenty-four starts to turn the vegetables limp. The salt itself rinses off in the colander before the cook, so the pickle doesn't taste salty in the jar.
attrs.title: Why the brine matters

content.text: What you need

items.name: Cauliflower
items.prepNote: broken into small florets, around 500 g prepared weight

content.text: A non-reactive pan is essential; vinegar reacts with plain aluminium and copper, which spoils the flavour and stains the pan. Stainless steel or a proper preserving pan is right. Sterilise the jars by washing them hot, drying them in a 120°C oven for fifteen minutes, and filling them while both jars and pickle are still warm.
Method
Brine the vegetables overnight

Mix {{water}} of cold water and {{sea-salt-fine}} of fine salt in a large bowl, stirring until the salt dissolves. Add the prepared cauliflower, courgette, green beans, shallots, and cucumber. Press the vegetables down under the surface of the brine with a plate. Cover and leave at room temperature for twelve hours, overnight.
Drain and rinse

Tip the brined vegetables into a colander and rinse under cold running water for two minutes. Shake the colander to drain off as much water as possible, then leave the vegetables in the colander while you make the sauce. The vegetables should taste of vegetable, not of salt; if a piece of cauliflower tastes salty, rinse again.
Mix the sauce paste

In a small bowl, mix {{english-mustard-powder}} of English mustard powder, {{turmeric}} of turmeric, {{ginger-ground}} of ground ginger, {{cornflour}} of cornflour, and a splash of vinegar from the measured amount. Stir to a smooth paste with no dry lumps. The paste should be the texture of double cream.
Heat the vinegar

Pour the rest of the {{malt-vinegar}} of malt vinegar into a large preserving pan. Add {{granulated-sugar}} of sugar, {{mustard-seeds-yellow}} of yellow mustard seeds, and {{coriander-seeds}} of crushed coriander seeds. Bring to a simmer over medium heat. Stir until the sugar dissolves.
Thicken the sauce

Ladle two or three spoonfuls of hot vinegar into the mustard paste, stirring as you go, to slacken it. Pour the slackened paste back into the pan, whisking constantly. The sauce will thicken to the texture of pourable custard in two or three minutes. Keep whisking; let it bubble for a minute to cook the cornflour through.
Add the vegetables

Tip the drained vegetables into the pan. Stir gently to coat every piece in the sauce. Bring back to a low simmer and cook for three minutes, no longer; the vegetables stay crisp this way. If the sauce looks too thick to spoon into jars, loosen with a splash of boiling water; if too thin, simmer a minute longer.
Jar while hot

Spoon the hot piccalilli into hot sterilised jars, pressing down to remove air pockets and leaving 1 cm headspace at the top. Wipe the rims clean with a piece of kitchen paper dipped in vinegar. Screw on vinegar-proof lids tightly. Label with the date.

Store the jars in a cool dark cupboard for three weeks before opening. The first jar tasted at three weeks is much better than the first jar tasted at three days; the mustard mellows, the vegetables drink up the sauce, the colour deepens. Sealed jars keep for six months on the shelf. Once opened, the jar goes in the fridge and keeps for a month.

attrs.intro: Most piccalilli problems show up later, in the jar, rather than at the cooking stage. The fixes mostly happen on the next batch.

items.fix: Brine for the full twelve hours and shake the colander hard. The brine pulls water out so the vegetables don't bleed into the sauce later. If the vegetables go in still wet, they release that water in storage.
items.cause: Brine too short or vegetables not drained well.
items.symptom: Sauce thinned to a soup three weeks into the jar.

items.fix: Three minutes is enough. The pickle keeps cooking gently in the jar for the first week as it cools; over-cook at the pan stage and the vegetables collapse. The cauliflower should still squeak when you eat it.
items.cause: Cooked too long at the sauce stage.
items.symptom: Vegetables limp and grey by the time the jar opens.

items.fix: Always slacken cornflour with cold vinegar first; the cold liquid lets the starch hydrate evenly. Hot vinegar straight onto dry cornflour gives lumps that won't whisk out.
items.cause: Cornflour added straight to hot vinegar.

items.fix: The slackening step matters. The paste needs to be loose before it joins the hot sauce; a stiff paste hits the hot vinegar and seizes. Heat the vinegar to a simmer, no hotter, when the paste goes in.
items.cause: Mustard powder seized into clumps; pan too hot when the paste went in.
items.symptom: Sauce separated, oily ring around the top of the jar.

items.fix: Throw the affected jar away; the metal contaminates the pickle and tastes bitter. Use proper preserving lids next time. Standard supermarket jam-jar lids work; very old metal lids often do not.
items.cause: Lid not vinegar-proof; metal corroding into the pickle.
items.symptom: Rust spots on the inside of the lid after a few weeks.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting

content.text: Variations

Marrow piccalilli is the autumn version when there's a glut: substitute peeled, deseeded marrow for the courgette in roughly the same weight. Runner beans can take the place of green beans in late summer; cut them into thumb-tip pieces. Sweeter palates can add another fifty grams of sugar; the bite of the mustard is what carries it, so don't drop the mustard amount even if you cut the sugar.
Make ahead, freezing, leftovers

Piccalilli is itself a make-ahead recipe; the whole point of pickling is shelf storage. Three weeks of maturation, six months unopened in a cool cupboard, a month in the fridge once opened. It doesn't freeze: the vegetables collapse and the cornflour-thickened sauce splits on thawing. If the cupboard has the room, make a double batch in autumn and you have piccalilli on the table all winter.
Where this dish lives

Piccalilli is what late-Victorian British cookery called Indian pickle. The Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton printed it under that name in 1861. The word piccalilli took over by the end of the 1800s. The yellow colour and the mustard heat point to its roots in the Anglo-Indian kitchens of the East India Company. British cooks adapted Indian achar recipes to the vegetables and vinegars they had at home.

The dish settled into the British pickle canon alongside pickled walnuts, pickled onions, and chutney, and stayed there. It lives on the cold table: with a Boxing Day ham, a pork pie at a pub lunch, a wedge of mature cheddar in a ploughman's, a slice of cold roast pork in a sandwich. Three weeks in the cupboard is the cost of admission, but the jar earns it back the moment it opens.

recipe.yieldDescription: about 1.5 kg; fills three 500 ml jars
